primary_topics: nuclear submarine proof load test as mechanical stress relief

cases_mentioned.name: Helms Pumped Storage Project penstock failure
cases_mentioned.frame: Penstock failure: American Bridge welded inch-and-quarter fillet weld with snow on the metal, no preheat, no post weld heat treatment; delayed hydrogen cracking after winter freeze; failure during first pressurization at 188 psi.
cases_mentioned.litigation: Fresno trial early 1990s; PG&E lost vs. American Bridge; American Bridge awarded $17M punitive damages for slander by a PG&E-hired metallurgist who pre-announced fault in the press.

cases_mentioned.name: Cooper Heat field stress relief
cases_mentioned.frame: Heat-treatment contractor that builds local furnaces around pressure vessels on-site; box furnace 118 feet long with railroad tracks for oil refinery vessel.

cases_mentioned.name: Nuclear submarine proof load test
cases_mentioned.frame: Submarines too large for thermal stress relief; mechanical stress relief via first deep dive; shipyard top management required to ride along as quality control.

cases_mentioned.name: Steinmetz consulting fee anecdote
cases_mentioned.frame: Steinmetz invoices GE $1,000 for chalk mark on misbehaving generator: $1 chalk, $999 knowing where to put the X.

cases_mentioned.name: John Wulff / Boston Edison consulting fee (incomplete)
cases_mentioned.frame: Wulff demands $5,000 fee before diagnosing corrosion of stainless steel in Boston Edison generating plant; cause traced to first Venezuelan oil shipment. Story cuts off mid-telling.

people_named: John Wulff (MIT metallurgy, captioner garble 'John Willis')
notes: Recording terminates at ~37:40 mid-John-Wulff anecdote. The Helms project anecdote is the centerpiece teaching case; it links the three Venn-diagram circles (residual stress, hardness, hydrogen) by demonstrating a real-world failure that violated all three preventive measures simultaneously. Captioner quality is notably poor on this transcript — recurring garbles of 'post weld heat treatment,' 'fillet weld,' 'Steinmetz,' and 'Wulff.'
